svg.text: AMERICAN SIGN LANGUAGE
HISTORY — DEAF CULTURE AND COMMUNITY
ORIGINS OF ASL
Martha's Vineyard, MA (~1700s): high hereditary deafness; whole community signed
1817: American School for the Deaf founded in Hartford, CT
Thomas Hopkins Gallaudet + Laurent Clerc (Deaf French signer) founded it
ASL = mix of Martha's Vineyard Sign + LSF (French Sign Language)
GALLAUDET UNIVERSITY
1864: Gallaudet University founded in Washington DC (Lincoln signed charter)
World's only liberal arts university for the Deaf; instruction in ASL
1988: Deaf President Now protest; first Deaf president selected (I. King Jordan)
ORALISM AND SUPPRESSION
1880: Milan Conference; hearing educators voted to ban sign language in schools
~100 years of oralism: Deaf children forced to speak, not sign
1960: Stokoe publishes "Sign Language Structure" — proves ASL is language
Resurgence: ASL recognized, Deaf culture reclaimed from ~1970s on
LSM HISTORY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
LSM ORIGINS
Mexico: first school for the Deaf founded 1867 in Mexico City
French Sign Language influence via French educational missionaries
LSM developed independently from ASL despite geographic proximity
LSM STANDARDIZATION
~100,000 LSM users estimated in Mexico
2005: Mexico officially recognizes LSM as national language
CELAM (Centro LSM) and FENASCOL: standardization efforts
DEAF CULTURE: SHARED VALUES
Deaf culture (capital D): identity, not disability
Both ASL and LSM communities: strong cultural identity, Deaf schools, Deaf clubs
Cross-border Deaf communities: sign language conferences, Deaflympics
Cochlear implant controversy: Deaf culture vs medical model of deafness
~500,000 ASL users in US • ~100,000 LSM users in Mexico
PLATE 62d ALT · ASL / LSM · HISTORY
